data.epi_content:
How many years had passed?

Since Evan first picked me up like a doll and shook me vigorously, we've gone through twelve cold winters, so it must have been twelve years.

For the first year, no matter how much love we shared, we couldn't conceive a child, making me wonder if I was infertile. But not long after, I gave birth to a daughter, and soon after, a son.

Nothing special happened.

Until now, we've somehow lived quite ordinarily.

When my daughter came back from the village after being bullied by other children, I didn't burn those children to death. And when three rough men from the village—a woodcutter and two hunters—tried to attack me, I merely castrated them and let them go.

Of course, I did silence them with a spell.

But while we were living so peacefully and ordinarily...

Why did this person come looking for me?

"It's been a while."

"Yes, it's been a while, Erica."

I heard someone knocking at the door and thought it might be the mother of a child I'd befriended bringing the eggs I'd asked for. But when I opened the door, a completely different and unexpected person was standing there.

I wouldn't have been as surprised if the three men whose testicles had been torn off had come for revenge.

Because Vivian was standing before me, wrapped in a snow-white robe.

"It's cold, why don't you come in first?"

"...Sure."

Vivian pushed back her hood, shook her head once, and entered the house.

"It's warm in here."

She sat on the sofa in front of the fireplace and began warming her hands.

"Evan has recently taken up a hobby of making such things.

The fire burning now isn't conjured by a spell, but charcoal fire."

At those words, Vivian frowned slightly as if displeased, then muttered quietly to herself.

"Why not use a spell?"

I seemed to understand what she meant by that question, so I answered lightly with a hint of mockery.

"Would you prefer that?"

Rather than answering, Vivian shook her head.

Then she took out a tobacco from her pocket and was lighting it.

I never thought she would smoke such things, it was unexpected.

"Children come and go here, I'd appreciate it if you put out the smoke."

"...Ah. Oh."

Vivian looked slightly dazed and cut off the tip of the tobacco she had just lit.

"Children live here?"

Her tone seemed to ask whether we deserved to have children and live so happily.

Or perhaps she was asking if I had kidnapped a child from the street to use as magical ingredients.

From the feeling I got, I thought it might be the latter.

So, as if making an excuse, I told her about my children.

"Two. One is cute like me, and one is both cute and handsome like Evan."

Crunch.

I heard a sound like teeth grinding.

But since Vivian was just smiling normally, I simply stared at her steadily rather than pointing it out.

In the silence that wasn't particularly awkward but filled with tension—not knowing when she might attack me—I fidgeted my fingers slightly.

Vivian didn't seem interested in fighting, as she spoke again.

"How enviable."

"Yes, but I doubt that's what you came to ask about."

I walked to the kitchen and brewed two cups of tea.

I didn't bother with foolishness like adding poison.

Vivian would probably know spells to detect any poison anyway.

It was green tea that had been fermented in a cool place for quite some time, until it turned dark, and then dried.
data.epi_content2:
"Drink up. You're a guest after all. I should offer some hospitality."

"How kind of you."

Vivian took the tea and, still unable to drink hot things well, blew on it before lightly touching it with her tongue and beginning to sip it slowly.

"So why did you come looking for me?"

"I was curious whether a terrible villain would eventually be punished as time passed."

A villain.

She burned to death.

If she's calling me a villain, isn't that too cruel?

I could easily make some hollow, disgusting excuse that I didn't want to act that way.

But any conscience that remained in my heart had fallen away long ago, on the day I became a roly-poly doll.

"You must be disappointed, as I'm living quite happily."

At those words, Vivian's hand trembled as she gripped the teacup tightly.

Simultaneously, with a crack, the handle broke off and hot tea spilled onto the floor.

"Erica, it's not all over yet.

Even now, if I were to take up my staff and turn your son and daughter to ashes, wouldn't that make for a common tale of poetic justice?"

"I wonder."

Suddenly my mouth felt dry.

Was it fear, or was it anger at seeing this magician—who bore no resemblance to her former self—threatening my family?

Rather than answering Vivian's threat, I went to the kitchen and poured plenty of milk into the tea I had prepared.

When I swallowed the tea again, the dry feeling seemed to subside.

"So, are you going to kill them?"

"I don't know. I've always pursued only what's right."

Vivian sighed and spoke in a slightly angry tone, almost growling.

Then she looked at me and, lifting one corner of her mouth as if mocking me, said:

"But it seems even you fear your children dying."

"Yes. I'm afraid."

"Haha."

Vivian was picking up the broken glass, just like she did long ago when she came to my room to pick up glass.

The glass seemed to have grazed her hand slightly, but she had calluses and didn't get cut.

"...You asked why I came, Erica?

I came hoping to see you and Evan living miserably. I thought it would be satisfying.

It would be truly hateful if you were living normally after leaving indelible scars on others."

I let out a hollow laugh.

"You know what? I found out that you two also killed the knight who raised me after my father passed away.

I learned that after you two ran away."

What's this, she just came to lament?

She must really have no one around to confide in.

She used to be surrounded by people, how did she fall so far?

If I tease her a bit, she'll probably leave on her own.

It would be nearly impossible for me to drive her away by force.

"Can you kill them?"

At those words, Vivian glared at me with gleaming eyes and retorted:

"You think I can't? Do you know how many pieces of trash like you fanatics I've killed..."

"Yes, you've killed trash. So you're going to kill our sweet, innocent children?"

I got up from my seat and approached Vivian, who was sitting by the fireplace.

She didn't show any signs of wariness. As if any foolish action would be useless.

"My children, including Evan, are my entire world.

If they die, I would probably follow them, weeping tears of blood.

But, Vivian. You absolutely cannot kill them. Because there's no next time for you."

I took out a pistol from my bosom and placed the barrel against my temple.
data.epi_content3:
Even if I have to sacrifice myself...

"That's why you let me go.

You were afraid that if you didn't punish the wicked, perhaps millions more would die, so you let me go.

What nonsense are you talking now, Vivian?"

From outside, I heard the sound of someone running and opening the door.

It might be my daughter returning after playing with friends.

I whispered so the child wouldn't hear:

With absolute certainty that Vivian would never, ever kill my child.

If it was Vivian, who had tormented me so much with her principles, she absolutely...

I could probably block an attack once or twice.

I could throw my body to shield them. And if she tries again?

I'll have to start over.

At least my children will survive here.

If I die, Evan will come running faster than anyone to protect our children.

"You can't kill them. Never."

Vivian snorted at my claim that she couldn't kill, but when she heard the "never" that followed, her trembling hands finally settled down.

"You're right."

And soon after, my daughter entered.

"Oh, someone I've never seen before! Mom, who is this!?"

"Someone I met a long time..."

While I was wondering what to call Vivian, she spoke with a blank face.

"A friend, a friend from long ago.

I'm envious that you're living well, Erica."

Then she stood up and walked out of the house.

I held my daughter's hand tightly and tried to calm my trembling hands.

When had they started shaking?

Looking outside through the window, I saw Vivian smoking tobacco.

"Kallia, go upstairs and wait in your room.

I'll make a snack and bring it to you soon."

My daughter nodded and, seemingly tired from playing, dragged her feet as she climbed the stairs to the upper floor.

I went to Vivian's side, took out a cigarette for the first time in a long while, and blew smoke beside her.

I don't know why she came.

Perhaps she came after experiencing heartbreak with a lover.

But Vivian was busy inhaling and exhaling smoke, with small tears flowing from her eyes, so I didn't speak to her.

By the time the cigarette had burned down completely, Vivian spoke quietly.

"I'm sorry for coming here for no reason. I won't come again."

It felt as if a ghost from the past was speaking, which gave me a slightly satisfied feeling.

"Yes, don't come again.

I'll let you visit my gravestone after I'm dead."

"Ha, there's no need."

With that, Vivian disappeared somewhere.

About two hours after I sent up the snack, Evan walked into the house with a flushed face, seemingly drunk after having drinks with a friend.

Today's kiss had an unpleasant alcohol scent.

"We had a visitor."

"Who?"

"Vivian."

"...Why?"

"I don't know. Anyway, I felt uneasy. Hold me."

At those words, Evan embraced me tightly.
